mainEntity.name: Does homeowners insurance cover earthquakes in California?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Earthquake damage is specifically excluded from standard California homeowners policies. To cover earthquake damage you need a separate earthquake insurance policy, available through the California Earthquake Authority (CEA) or private carriers.

mainEntity.name: How much does earthquake insurance cost in California?

acceptedAnswer.text: A typical California homeowner pays roughly $800 to $3,000+ per year depending on location, home construction, deductible, and coverage limits. Homes near active fault lines or on soft soil are at the higher end.

mainEntity.name: Is private earthquake insurance better than the CEA?

acceptedAnswer.text: Often, yes. Private carriers can offer deductibles as low as 2.5–5% of dwelling coverage versus the CEA's typical 10–25%, plus higher limits and broader personal property coverage. An independent agent representing both markets can run a real side-by-side comparison.

mainEntity.name: What deductible does California earthquake insurance have?

acceptedAnswer.text: Earthquake deductibles are a percentage of your dwelling coverage, not a flat dollar amount. CEA policies typically run 5–25%. On a $600,000 home with a 15% deductible, you pay the first $90,000 of damage out of pocket.
